title: Measuring Innovation and Product Differentiation: Evidence from Mutual Funds
abstract: We study innovation and product differentiation using a uniqueness measure based on textual analysis of prospectuses. We find that small and start-up families have higher start rates than larger families, and their products are more unique. Unique strategies attract more inflows in the first three years, and investors respond more to text-based uniqueness than other measures such as holdings or returns uniqueness. For established funds, word uniqueness has weak negative power for explaining returns, so investors in competitive equilibrium do not sacrifice much performance to get specialized products. Uniqueness attenuates the flow-performance relation, reducing the risk of investor outflows.

article.introduction:
In this paper, we study entry, innovation, and product differentiation by U.S. actively managed equity mutual funds. From 1975 to 2016, the number of mutual funds, excluding money market funds, rose from 530 with $45 billion in assets to 7,736 with $13.4 trillion in assets. The number of fund families (i.e., fund sponsors) increased from 297 to 824 during the same period, and the number of Morningstar style classifications rose from fewer than 20 to over 100, nearly tripling in the past 20 years. The proliferation of funds and fund styles raises important questions about how existing fund families and potential sponsors of new fund families make product introduction decisions, and the consequences of such decisions for investor flows. While these questions have received attention in prior literature (see, for example, Khorana and Servaes (1999,2012), Massa (2000,2003), Caffrey (2006), Wahal and Wang (2011), Hoberg, Kumar, and Prabhala (2018)), many issues remain unexplored.
We first focus on how to measure innovation and product differentiation. Next, we examine the causes and consequences of differentiation, both for new funds and for established funds. The power of our approach comes in part from the use of text-based measures. The motivation for using such measures is strong. For any given fund, the correlation of its securities’ measurable characteristics (e.g., size, book to market, returns) with other funds, particularly those of the same style, is likely to be high, in which case differences in standard characteristics will not fully capture the relevant degree of “uniqueness.” If fund sponsors want to create a fund that is viewed as “new” and “different,” they could use the prospectus, in particular, the written description of the fund, to differentiate their product and reduce search costs for clienteles with different tastes for fund attributes.
To illustrate, consider the Patriot Fund, which opened in 2012. The Patriot Fund invests primarily in common stocks included in the S&P 500 index that pass the fund's “patriotic” investment screen, which attempts to weed out companies that do business with terror-sponsoring nations as defined by the U.S. Department of State. Based on standard financial measures such as holdings’ characteristics, this fund looks like (and is classified by Morningstar as) a Large Cap Blend fund. However, the text-based uniqueness of the Patriot Fund is more than two standard deviations higher than that of a typical Large Cap Blend fund. This high level of uniqueness makes sense, and suggests that an investor would be unable to replicate the fund's investment strategy with any other existing mutual fund.
The paper proceeds in three steps. First, we develop a text-based measure of a fund's uniqueness and compare it to holdings and return-based measures. Validation tests show that the text-based measure contains information that predicts some aspects of fund strategy better than other measures, which generalizes the intuition provided by the Patriot Fund example. We thus use text-based and other differentiation measures to address a broad set of questions.
Second, we study new funds. Previous papers typically define innovation as a fund start (e.g., Sirri and Tufano (1993)), and use the terms “innovation” and “product differentiation” interchangeably. While there is a positive correlation between thenumberof new funds introduced and family size, we document that therateat which large families offer new funds (e.g., the percentage of their funds that are new funds) is lower than for other families, including new families. The start rate for families in the lowest (first) size quintile is 168% higher than that for families in the highest (fifth) quintile. On average, the family size of a new fund offering is 41.3% ($47.5 billion) smaller than that of an existing fund in the same month. Furthermore, since there are more small than large families, this size distribution reinforces the view that innovation comes from smaller (and new) families, which runs counter to previous literature (e.g., Khorana and Servaes (1999)).
Our text-based measure uses ex ante reported strategies available for investors prior to the fund's inception. We find that smaller families and new families not only offer more new products, but offer more unique products than larger existing families. These findings make sense because we do not expect small families to be able to compete based on economies of scale or brand recognition. Small and new fund families charge more (i.e., have higher expense ratios) on their more innovative new funds. For a fund offered by a new family, a one-standard-deviation shock to text-based uniqueness is associated with an approximately 12 basis point (bp) increase in annual expense ratio. Large families charge less (have lower expense ratios) on unique funds than small families. However, firms (large and small) earn higher total dollar fees on unique funds after the inception date.
More innovative offerings attract more assets under management in the first few years of operation. Funds with above-median uniqueness attract about 50% (approximately $75 million) more in asset flows than less innovative (below-median uniqueness) new funds in the first 36 months of operation. Text-based uniqueness is a significant independent predictor of fund flows for recently opened funds, even after using other uniqueness measures and controlling for expenses, which include marketing expenses. These results indicate that investors pay attention to the uniqueness of the underlying product revealed in the text of the prospectus.
The third part of our study focuses on established funds. It seems unlikely that a unique fund can attract new money forever in a competitive market. For example, competing products may come along, or there might be a limited market for highly innovative strategies. We examine product uniqueness in an industry equilibrium setting, reflecting in part the intuition of Berk and Green (2004). In their model, flow follows alpha and net alpha is driven to zero because of increasing returns to scale. In our setting, investors care about net alpha as well as uniqueness. However, with clientele effects and a limited market for a unique strategy, existing unique funds can survive even if they do not generate alpha. Furthermore, some investors may be willing to sacrifice net performance to get characteristics they value.
Our empirical tests study the relation between flows, uniqueness, and alpha for established funds. Fund returns show only a weak negative relation with text-based uniqueness. Overall, offering a fund that uses a unique strategy with popular appeal allows a firm to initially collect rents from its first-mover advantage, but competition eliminates such rents fairly quickly.
We also find that unique products have significantly lower flow-performance sensitivity than other funds. One possible explanation for this finding is that due to less product competition for more unique funds, it is more difficult for investors to react to underperformance by liquidating their holdings and putting their money in a comparable product offered by another firm. If there is a limited market for highly innovative strategies, outperformance will not attract as many new investors. An alternative explanation is that more unique products are harder to benchmark. Ferson and Lin (2014) note that client-specific alphas can be very different from the alphas used in academic research due to incomplete markets and investor disagreement. In any case, fund sponsors gain an additional benefit from offering unique products because they are less susceptible to the costs associated with flow shocks.
Our analysis of innovation and differentiation fills an important gap. We provide new evidence from the asset management industry that small organizations drive financial innovation. Frame and White (2004) find surprisingly few studies on financial innovation, and only two that look at the conditions that lead to financial innovation. Lerner (2006) tries to fill this gap by collecting data on financial innovations fromWall Street Journalarticles. Consistent with our results, he finds that smaller and less profitable firms drive innovation to a large extent. Furthermore, our sample is extensive. We use the synopsis of the “Principal Investment Strategies” (PIS) section of the prospectus available for all funds on the Morningstar Principia CDs. Our sample also includes non-U.S. equity funds and hedged funds for which holdings data are often incomplete or unavailable because much of the strategic innovation in the mutual fund industry over the last two decades has been in these categories. Other mutual fund studies using text-based analysis are scarce and do not focus on innovation. Daughdrill (2014) uses a smaller sample of 237 funds, and Hillert, Niessen-Ruenzi, and Ruenzi (2016) study how the writing style of shareholder letters affects flows and performance.
The paper is organized as follows. SectionIlays out the paper's framework and hypotheses. SectionIIdiscusses our data, including the uniqueness measures. SectionsIIIandIVpresent the main results. Finally, SectionVconcludes. Additional details on the text-based measure are provided in the Appendices.
article.I. Framework and Hypotheses:
Our framework assumes that fund sponsors are rational profit maximizers, and that there is competition for investor funds. Investors face search costs and have different tastes for different portfolio attributes. Below, we first develop testable propositions about new funds and differentiation (Sections I.A through I.C). We then broaden the analysis to all funds and consider product differentiation in an industry equilibrium setting (Section I.D).
Large families benefit from both economies of scale and brand recognition. Thus, based on the number of new fund starts, it is not surprising to find as in Khorana and Servaes (1999, p. 1072) that it is large families that tend to innovate. However, our analysis of new fund starts goes further by studying therateat which families of various size offer new funds (e.g., the percentage of all funds in a given family size group that are new).
There is more to innovation than new starts. Since small and new families cannot easily compete on expenses and reputation, we argue that the main dimension on which they compete is product uniqueness. We predict that the new products that small families offer tend to be more differentiated than those of large families. While the predicted relation should hold for a variety of differentiation measures, it should be strongest for the text-based measure as we discuss below.
The economics literature has long recognized the role of heterogeneous consumer tastes and product differentiation in imperfectly competitive markets (e.g., Chamberlin (1933), Robinson (1933)). In contrast to previous papers in the literature that use data on a fund's securities holdings to capture differentiation (see, for example, Wahal and Wang (2011), Khorana and Servaes (2012), Blocher (2016), and Hoberg, Kumar, and Prabhala (2018)), we use textual analysis of the mutual fund's prospectus.
Our text-based approach is motivated by the marketing literature, which sees product differentiation as a strategy to generate a competitive advantage for firms that are unable to compete purely on cost (Porter (1980)). Such firms market their products as “different” and “better” along one or more dimensions that consumers potentially value, with these dimensions not necessarily material or related to the product's primary purpose.1In our context, mutual funds can use prospectus text to convey information about fund properties that cannot be easily inferred from observing holdings or returns.
In addition, using text to capture investor attention and raise the visibility or “salience” of a fund can attract clients in the presence of search frictions. Salience refers to how an item stands out (in the marketing literature, see, for example, Ehrenberg, Barnard, and Scriven (1997) and Romaniuk and Sharp (2004)). Words in a prospectus text are easier to process and can be a more salient, visible product differentiator than holdings or returns data. In the finance literature, the importance of an individual stock's visibility for investor behavior is demonstrated in, for example, Gervais, Kaniel, and Mingelgrin (2001) and Barber and Odean (2008).
Investor demand for differentiated mutual funds can arise for various reasons. For example, an investor may simply have a taste for a particular type of firm in the fund (e.g., “environmentally friendly”), independent of the return distribution. Alternatively, investors may have a (possibly mistaken) belief, or be convinced through advertising, that a particular fund characteristic or strategy can make a fund outperform or have a more desirable pattern of upside or downside risk (see Polkovnichenko, Wei, and Zhao (2019)).
Our framework is related to Hortacsu and Syverson (2004), whose model assumes that portfolio holdings across funds are identical (i.e., S&P 500 index funds). Investors in their model face search costs and have preferences for attributes other than portfolio composition. In our framework, portfolio holdings are not identical, even within a style. We argue that with search costs, subtle and potentially important differences in uniqueness are investor-relevant attributes not easily observable from holdings (or returns) data.
The empirical relation between new fund uniqueness and flows provides evidence on investor response and represents a key test of the paper's story. Unique new funds should have higher flows, after controlling for fees. Moreover, the relation between flows and fees should be strongest for text-based uniqueness measures, to the extent that these are relevant to investors.
We also study expense ratios and total dollar fees received from investors. Unique new products have a first-mover advantage and can command a higher percentage fee if sponsors collect rents. Given the greater brand recognition of large families, it is also of interest to see how they exploit this element of product differentiation when they offer new funds. To shed light on this question, we also examine total dollar fees received (i.e., assets under management multiplied by the percentage fee). Rates (i.e., percentage fees) are set to maximize any rents. A low rate brings in more money, and a family with more brand recognition can potentially draw higher flows for a given percentage fee.
New funds are a perturbation to equilibrium. To examine uniqueness in an equilibrium setting, we extend our analysis to the cross-section of all funds. New funds with no track record can attract flows because they are unique. It seems unlikely, however, that a unique fund can attract flows forever in a competitive market. Despite any first-mover advantage, flow-inducing product uniqueness can be replicated. Our analysis first examines the time path of flow to new funds and how long abnormal flows last for a new fund.
We next examine the performance of established funds and how this relates to product uniqueness. A starting point is the intuition of Berk and Green (2004). In their model, investors care only about fund net alpha (e.g., abnormal performance), and flows follow alpha. Positive-alpha funds grow but eventually their net alpha is driven to zero because of increasing returns to scale. Like Berk and Green (2004), our analysis allows for differential alpha, which should be driven to zero over time, but we also incorporate uniqueness.
We examine uniqueness for the cross-section of funds in two ways. First, we examine alpha for existing funds and the relation to uniqueness. The link to Berk and Green (2004) is simple. If uniqueness is sufficiently important to investors,existingfunds can survive even if they do not generate alpha. Furthermore, due to clientele effects, some investors could be willing to sacrifice performance to get characteristics not reflected in alpha.
Second, we account for the fact that differentiation has potential consequences for the flow-performance relation and hence for the firm's decision to offer unique products. For a differentiated product, it is more difficult to do performance benchmarking as by definition there are fewer perfect substitutes. Product uniqueness should therefore attenuate the flow-performance relation. This should be the case for all uniqueness measures but should be most pronounced using text-based uniqueness measures, given “brand loyalty” for established funds. Such loyalty reflects habit formation at the product level, which reduces competition and forms a buffer against negative economic shocks (van Binsbergen (2016)). If benchmarking is more difficult for highly unique funds, however, it is difficult to distinguish between the brand loyalty and benchmarking explanations.
A buffer against negative flow shocks provides sponsors an incentive to offer unique products because it lowers the potential for redemptions that result in asset sales at fire-sale prices (see Coval and Stafford (2007)). Furthermore, brand loyalty implies that investors pay less attention to performance if the fund benchmark is misspecified (see Sensoy (2009) for a discussion of fund-designated benchmark misspecification).
article.II. Data and Validation Tests:
The main data sources for this paper are the CRSP Survivor-Bias-Free U.S. Mutual Fund Database (CRSP) and annual Morningstar Principia CDs (Morningstar). Both data sources include all funds available at the time (including currently defunct funds) and therefore are not affected by survivorship bias. Our sample consists of all actively managed equity mutual funds operating in the United States from 1999 to 2016, including diversified domestic equity funds, sector equity funds, international/global equity funds, and alternative/hedged equity funds. We construct this sample by merging CRSP and Morningstar, using CUSIP codes, ticker symbols, and, when CUSIP codes and ticker symbols are missing, manually matching by fund names. We eliminate index funds by removing all funds with the words “index,” “S&P,” “Dow Jones,” and “NASDAQ,” in the fund name, and by excluding all funds in the Dimensional Fund Advisors (DFA), Direxion, Potomac, ProFunds, and Rydex fund families. The fund names in these five families do not contain the keywords we associate with index funds, but they typically attempt to mimic the return of a benchmark or its inverse or leveraged versions. Exchange-traded funds and variable annuity underlying funds are also excluded based on theet_flagandvau_fundvariables in CRSP, as is standard practice in the mutual fund literature.
We aggregate fund variables across fund classes using the Morningstar portfolio identifier (PORTCODE) or MFLinks variable (WFICN), leaving one observation per portfolio-month. We remove incubated funds by excluding funds that were not contemporaneously reported in Morningstar or that had a blank fund name in CRSP at the start of the calendar year. This yields 636,628 portfolio-month observations during our sample period.
The fund pursues long-term capital appreciation in the mid-cap sector while seeking to provide the risk characteristics of the less volatile large-cap S&P 500® Index. The fund invests in a select group of mid-cap companies believed by the subadviser to be undervalued relative to their future growth potential. The investment strategy emphasizes companies believed by the subadviser to have a competitive advantage, strong management and low financial risk and to be able to grow over market cycles, despite their discounted valuations. Under normal circumstances, the fund invests at least 80% of its assets in equity securities of medium capitalization companies that, at the time of initial purchase, have market capitalizations of between $1 billion and $10 billion. Generally, the fund invests in 25–35 securities at any given time.
The Morningstar Advanced Analytics package in the annual Morningstar Principia CDs typically provides the entire text from this section of the prospectus, or if it is overly lengthy, a portion of it.2The average text length for a fund in the Morningstar data set is 70 words. AppendixAprovides five examples of such descriptions for funds with high uniqueness and five examples for funds with low uniqueness.
For each fund's word array, we format the text by removing all characters other than the 26 English letters, replacing capital letters with lower-case letters, and deleting common suffixes such as “-s,” “-ed,” “-ly,” and “-ing.” We then filter the text to reduce the amount of noise unrelated to the fund's strategy. AppendixBdiscusses the filtering procedures in detail.3Figure1contains a word cloud that highlights the most common informative words, after the formatting and filtering steps, with the size of each word indicating its frequency.
We next calculate the overlap in words with each other fund in the same Morningstar category in the same year. For instance, if Fund A has 50 words in its array, and Fund B in the same category-year also includes 20 of those words in its array, the pairwise overlap (for Fund A) between A and B would be 20/50 or 0.4. For each fund-year observation, we then average this pairwise overlap measure across all other funds in the same category-year to calculate the variable,Text: Average Overlap.
TableIpresents summary statistics forText: Average Overlap. Panel A shows that the typical fund shares 39.9% of words with other funds in the same Morningstar category, with significant variation—the overlap ranges between 28.2% (at the 10thpercentile) and 50.4% (at the 90thpercentile). In Panel B, we find that the textual overlap for new funds is lower (median of 38.7%) than that for all funds, but this difference is not statistically significant. Panel C reports uniqueness by subperiod. Average overlap has increased from 35% to 42% from the first third of the sample period (2000 to 2004) to the last third (2011 to 2015), possibly due to standardization of strategies or the language used to describe them. Finally, as we would expect, textual overlap is lower for funds that use niche strategies: hedged funds, funds of funds, and socially responsible investment (SRI) funds. We examine this last result more formally in a validation test in Section II.D.
We also analyze the readability and tonality of the PIS text and report the results in AppendixC. Funds with lower values of textual overlap use more complex language (i.e., have lower readability) and a greater number of words to describe their strategy. In Section II.C, we control for the number of words in the PIS when defining text-based uniqueness to ensure that our results are not affected by these differences. Hillert, Niessen-Ruenzi, and Ruenzi (2016) find that the tone of words in letters to fund shareholder can help explain future flows. We find that funds with lower values of textual overlap use more positive words but also more negative words. The incidence of tonal words is also very low in the section of the prospectus that we study (less than 3%) compared to shareholder letters, which typically analyze the market environment and explain past fund performance.4
To compare textual uniqueness to uniqueness in fund fundamentals (as in Wahal and Wang (2011), Hoberg, Kumar, and Prabhala (2018)), we define and standardize two additional measures based on portfolio holdings and fund returns. Our mutual fund holdings data come from two sources, namely, Thomson Reuters s12 and CRSP. Thomson Reuters has holdings for our entire sample period but only provides long positions in domestic equities. CRSP holdings data are available only after 2003, but starting in 2008 contain comprehensive data on all holdings (long and short positions), including cash, bonds, derivatives, and international stocks. To have the broadest possible coverage, we use Thomson Reuters for pre-2008 holdings and CRSP for holdings in 2008 and thereafter.
For each fund-year observation, we take the last set of holdings that the fund reported in the given year. We then calculate the cosine similarity5of holdings with each other fund in the same Morningstar category-year. We average this pairwise measure across all other funds in the category-year to calculateHoldings: Average Cosine Similarity. TableIpresents summary statistics for this measure. The typical fund has only 19.5% of holdings in common with other funds in the same style. This low average value highlights the degrees of freedom that mutual fund managers have in choosing portfolio assets that meet their fund style and objective. It also points to a potential problem with the use of holdings to measure uniqueness: due to the large number of available assets, a fund that uses a generic strategy can still hold a portfolio that is very different from those of its peers.
Panel C of TableIshows that holdings similarity has declined over our sample period, opposite of the result for textual overlap. This discrepancy can be explained by the change in our holdings data source in 2008, from Thomson Reuters to CRSP, which allows us to include hedged funds and international funds in our sample in later years. Those styles also happen to have more available (investable) assets and thus lower holdings similarity with other funds in the same style, which reduces the sample average in later years. The results for average holdings overlap for new funds (in Panel B) and different fund styles (in Panel D) are broadly consistent with those for average textual overlap.
We also construct a returns-based measure of similarity. For each fund-year observation, we calculate the correlation of daily returns with each other fund in the same Morningstar category-year. We then average this pairwise measure across all other funds in the same category-year to calculateReturns: Average Correlation. TableIpresents summary statistics for this measure. The average value of this measure in the sample is 0.884, much higher than what we find for textual overlap and holdings similarity. This result is due to the dominant role of factor exposure in explaining variation in returns. Funds in the same style are likely to have similar factor loadings and thus highly correlated returns. Panel D shows that hedged funds, which have low factor loadings and focus on generating alpha, are a significant outlier, with an average return correlation of 0.524 with other funds in the same category.
Additional fund characteristics are collected from Morningstar and CRSP. Fund size is calculated as the sum of assets under management of all classes of the fund at the end of the prior month, as reported by CRSP. The fund's family is based on its advisor, as reported by CRSP at the end of the prior quarter. Family size is the sum of assets under management ofallfunds (including the nonequity funds and index funds that are not included in our analysis) with the same fund advisor. Fund age and fund starts are based on the fund's first offer date, as reported by CRSP. Expense ratio is also provided by CRSP. Fund category classifications come from Morningstar. Morningstar also reports whether a fund invests in other funds (fund of funds) or is a socially responsible fund (SRI). Tax-aware funds are defined based on the following phrases in the fund name:tax-sensitive,tax-aware,tax-efficient,tax-smart,tax-managed, andtax-optimized. The proportion of assets in institutional classes is based on the institutional class indicator variable in CRSP.
To eliminate the effect of outliers, we winsorizeFlows(%) at the 1% and 99% levels.
In the tests in which the dependent variable is the flow or fees after inception, we control for the prior performance of the fund. In these tests, we measure prior performance as the simple average of monthly returns over the prior 60 months if the fund has such a track record, or the average of all past monthly returns if the fund is less than 60 months old.
TableIIpresents summary statistics for fund variables, with cross-sectional statistics for new funds in Panel A and time-series averages of cross-sectional statistics for all funds in Panel B. The total number of starts from January 1999 to December 2015 is 4,245 (or approximately 250 per year). Figure2shows the number of starts each year. During the sample period, the annual number of starts has remained stable, except for two troughs in the aftermath of the stock market declines of 2001 to 2002 and 2008 to 2009. The average family size of a new fund is $62.1 billion, which is much lower than the corresponding $123 billion average for all funds in Panel B. The average expense ratio for new funds is 1.46%, which is slightly higher than the corresponding 1.33% for all funds in Panel B. The average fund has 43.5% of its assets in institutional shares at the end of its first calendar year of operation. However, this figure disguises the bimodal distribution with peaks at zero (retail-only) and one (institutional-only).
We construct the three measures of uniqueness by multiplying each of the corresponding similarity measures from TableIby negative one (since uniqueness is the opposite of similarity) and then standardizing them to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one. This simplifies interpretation of the regression coefficients and facilitates easy comparisons of coefficients. Finally, we regress the standardized text-based uniqueness measure on the number of words in the PIS description and define the residual asText Uniqueness. This ensures that any effects of uniqueness are independent of effects from the number of words used in the text. Similarly, we regress the standardized holdings-based uniqueness measure on the number of holdings and define the residual asHoldings Uniqueness.
Panel B of TableIIreports time-series averages of cross-sectional summary statistics for the entire sample of equity mutual funds. The average fund's assets under management during the sample period are approximately $1.2 billion, while the average age of a fund is greater than 12 years. Average monthly fund flows are 0.5% of assets but are significantly skewed, with the median fund suffering outflows of 0.29% of assets. Returns (net of expenses) are 0.49% per month during this period, and gross-of-expense returns average a slightly larger 0.60%.
In this subsection, we conduct a battery of tests to examine the validity of our text-based measure of uniqueness. We check that the text-based measure correlates sensibly with fund strategy, and we examine whether the text-based measure adds power in predicting fund strategy after controlling for measures derived from returns and holdings fundamentals. TableIIIpresents the results.
In Panel A of TableIII, we create indicator variables corresponding to groups of unique funds that are outside of Morningstar's classification system (categories), and we regress these indicator variables on the three uniqueness measures, fund-level control variables (from Panel B of TableII), and category-by-month fixed effects. In the first column of Panel A, the dependent variable isAll Unique Types, which is the maximum value of the five dependent variables used in columns (2) through (6). These five dummy variables are based on the following five categories: tax-aware funds, socially responsible funds, funds of funds, frontier markets,6and frontier sectors.7
Panel A of TableIIIhighlights the advantage of using text-based measures of uniqueness over fundamentals-based measures. In column (1),Text Uniquenesssignificantly correlates with the dummy variable corresponding to unique types (with at-statistic of 7.24), while neitherHoldings UniquenessnorReturns Uniquenesshas a significant association with unique types. The economic effect is also significant, with a one-standard-deviation increase inText Uniquenessincreasing the chance that a fund is a unique type by 3.5% (compared to 1.5% forHoldings Uniquenessand 0.3% forReturns Uniqueness).
The remaining columns of Panel A show that text-based uniqueness is especially useful for detecting tax-aware funds, socially responsible funds, and funds of funds. Funds of these types do not have very unique holdings (other than funds of funds, for which the holdings data are often unavailable), and their returns are actuallymorecorrelated with other funds. For funds in the frontier markets and frontier sectors categories, which can hold a very specific set of stocks, text-based uniqueness is similar in explanatory power to holdings-based and returns-based uniqueness measures. In summary, Panel A of TableIIIshows thatText Uniquenesscorresponds to fund classifications as intuition would suggest, and that it is superior at predicting unique types than the two fundamentals-based uniqueness measures.
In Panel B, we examine whether (and which) uniqueness measures can predict theR2from daily regressions of fund returns on the market premium, SMB, HML, and MOM factors (Fama and French (1993), Carhart (1997)). For each fund-year, we calculate theR2from regressing daily fund returns (in excess of the risk-free rate) on the four factors during that year. We then use theR2as the dependent variable in regressions on fund-level uniqueness measures, fund controls, and category-by-month fixed effects. Theory suggests that the four factors should have less explanatory power (i.e., lowerR2) for more unique funds.
We report the results in Panel B of TableIII. In columns (1) through (3), each of the three uniqueness measures is included alone as an explanatory variable. Consistent with intuition, unique funds have a larger idiosyncratic component explaining their returns (i.e., lowerR2), with this relation holding for each measure of uniqueness. In columns (4) and (5) of Panel B, we include all three measures in the regression simultaneously, to test whether text-based uniqueness continues to have explanatory power after controlling for the fundamentals-based uniqueness measures. Since the dependent variable,R2, is derived from daily returns,Returns Uniqueness(which is also derived from daily returns) is mechanically tied to it and thus has the strongest explanatory power. However,Text Uniquenesscontinues to have explanatory power in this multivariate setting, with an economic significance similar to that ofHoldings Uniquenessand with a highert-statistic. Thus, Panel B confirms that our text-based measure is significantly correlated with fund fundamentals with the expected sign, and shows that this result holds even after controlling for other measures.
In Panels C and D of TableIII, we conduct validation tests using a slightly different methodology. Instead of condensing similarity by calculating theaveragepairwise measure across all other funds in the same category-year, we use the sample of all fund pairs in the same year to investigate whether pairwise similarity in text (or holdings or returns) explains whether a pair of funds shares a particular feature. While the number of observations is large (over 100 million) since the number of pairs is related to the square of the number of funds, we cluster standard errors by the fund family of each fund in a pair, as well as by time, to ensure that thet-statistics we report are correct.
In Panel C, the dependent variable isSame Family, a dummy variable indicating whether the pair of funds is in the same fund family, while in Panel D the dependent variable isSame Category, a dummy variable indicating whether the pair of funds is in the same Morningstar category. The validation test in Panel C is designed to indicate whether the PIS text is based on the writing style of the individuals that produce this text for the fund family, and not just fundamentals. The positive coefficient onText: Overlapin column (1) is consistent with a family-driven writing style. However, we see similar positive and significant coefficients onHoldings: Cosine Similarityin column (2) and onReturns Correlationin column (3), indicating that funds in the same family are also more likely to have common fundamentals. The positive coefficient onText: Overlapcan be independently picking this up result as well.
Next, we include all three uniqueness measures in the regressions in columns (4) through (6) of Panel C. The coefficient onReturns Correlation(after controlling forHoldings: Cosine Similarity) turns negative. This negative sign suggests that fund families do not offer multiple funds with similar returns (e.g., two Small-Cap China funds), perhaps to avoid cannibalizing investor capital flows to the family. The coefficients on text-based and holdings-based similarity remain positive and significant. Because two funds in the same family are more likely to invest in the same stocks, they could also be more likely to use the same strategies (e.g., socially responsible investing), which are picked up by the text but not holdings or returns. As a result, we are unable to conclusively determine whether common strategies, common writing style, or both explain the positive relation in this panel betweenText: OverlapandSame Family.
In Panel D, we examine whether similar text, holdings, and/or returns predict two funds being in the same Morningstar category. Not surprisingly, in columns (1) through (3), each of the three measures of similarity predictsSame Category, with highly statistically significant coefficients. When we include all three measures of overlap together in the same regressions, all three coefficients remain positive and significant. The strongest predictor by far isHoldings: Cosine Similarity, which is not surprising since Morningstar categorizes funds into styles based on their holdings, not their returns or their PIS text. Nonetheless, this set of tests validates our text-based measure by showing that it helps predictSame Category, even after controlling for fundamentals-based measures.
article.III. Main Results: Mutual Fund Starts:
We first assess whether large or small families drive innovation in the mutual fund industry, by examining which types of families are responsible for more fund starts and for more unique new funds. Before we can conduct this analysis, we need to answer an important methodological question: what is the right way to measure fund starts? Khorana and Servaes (1999,2012) look at fund openings in a different sample period and find that large families open more funds (per period) than small families. This is an important result but it is not particularly surprising due to the significant fixed costs of opening a new fund that larger fund sponsors can more easily handle.
Instead, our analysis focuses on whether small or large fund families start new funds at a higherrate. Consider, for example, one large family that opens two funds and eight small families, which altogether have the same total number of funds as the large family, that each open one fund.
The small families would each open half the number of funds as the large family but at the same time would be responsible for eight of 10, or 80%, of the new funds. Our methodology gains additional support from recent trends in the mutual fund industry, as capital has flowed from small and midsized families to very large families (e.g., Vanguard, Blackrock) due to the increased importance of low fees and the related popularity of passive strategies. Our results could thus shed new light on whether such concentration of capital would spur or curtail aggregate innovation in asset management strategies.
We start by sorting families into quintiles based on their assets under management (with each quintile having the same number offunds)8and calculating the start rate for each family-month observation. The start rate is defined as the number of new funds started by a family that month divided by the number of existing funds in the family in the prior month. We annualize this figure by multiplying by 12 and present the average start rates for each quintile in Figure3, Panel A. Families in the first quintile, managing the 20% of funds with the smallest family size, have an average start rate of 5.2% per year. In other words, they start approximately one new fund each year for each 20 preexisting funds they manage. In contrast, families in the fifth quintile, managing the 20% of funds with the largest family size, have an average start rate of 1.9% per year. They start approximately one new fund each year for each 50 preexisting funds they manage. The 95% confidence bands confirm that the difference in start rates between these extreme quintiles is statistically significant.
In Figure3, Panel B, we separately display the start rates for the five family size quintiles for two subperiods, 1999 to 2007 and 2008 to 2015. This figure shows that during our sample period, the disproportionate role of small families in fund starts has only gotten stronger over time. In the second half of the sample period, the start rate by families in the smallest quintile was 5.6%, compared to only 1.2% for families in the largest quintile.
We also compare the average family size of new funds with the average family size of preexisting funds in the same month. For each newly opened fund, we take its preinceptionFamily Size, which is equal to the assets under management of the fund family that opened the fund in the month before the fund was opened. For new families, we set this variable to zero. In the same month, for each fund start observation, we also calculate the average family size across all existing funds and denote this matched family size. In Panel A of TableIV, we compare the average family size of new funds to the average matched family size across the 4,245 fund starts from 1999 to 2015. We find that families that start new funds are $47.5 billion (41.3%) smaller than those that operate existing funds at the same time. These differences are statistically significant at the 1% level. In Panel B, we restrict the sample to traditional domestic diversified funds and again find economically and statistically significant differences in family size of $38.7 billion (37.3%).
One could argue that the inclusion of new families, with family size of zero, makes for an unfair “horse race,” since there is a limited pool of existing fund management companies but a potentially limitless number of entrepreneurs could launch a new fund management company and start offering funds. Thus, in Panel C of TableIV, we only include funds started by preexisting families. The differences in family size are smaller, at $30.9 billion (26.2%), but are still statistically significant at the 1% level.
Starting new funds may not necessarily be indicative of innovation if those funds are not differentiated from existing products. Therefore, in Panel D of TableIVwe examine whether the uniqueness of new funds opened by smaller (and new) families is different from the uniqueness of new funds started by large existing families. In columns (1) through (3), we regress each of the three uniqueness measures on aNew Familydummy variable. The coefficients onNew Familyare positive and significant in all three specifications, indicating that new families start funds that are more unique based on text, holdings, and returns, with the effect on returns being the strongest.
In column (4) of Panel D, the dependent variable isText Uniqueness, but we also control for fundamentals-based uniqueness measures and include category-by-year fixed effects. TheNew Familydummy continues to have a significant positive coefficient, indicating that new families open funds that have more text-based uniqueness after controlling for holdings and returns uniqueness. In columns (5) and (6), we replaceNew FamilywithLog Family Sizeas the explanatory variable of interest. The coefficient onLog Family Sizeis negative in both columns, indicating that larger families start less unique funds, but is not statistically significant in column (6), suggesting that the key difference is between new and existing families rather than between small and large families. Overall, TableIVdemonstrates that new and small families open mutual funds at a higher rate, and that the funds they open are more unique, which means that they are primarily responsible for the proliferation of new products in the mutual fund industry.
Next, we investigate the economic forces that motivate fund sponsors to start unique funds. Strategic innovation is risky since unproven strategies could fail to generate returns or to attract a critical mass of investors for long-term survival. However, as described in SectionI, there is a potential first-mover advantage to starting a unique fund, which the sponsor can use to collect rents in the form of higher advisory fees. In this section, we examine how uniqueness relates to the initial setting of expense ratios (in percent) and to the evolution of collected fees (in dollars) over time.
We start with the sample of new funds, regressing their initial (first-reported) expense ratio onText Uniqueness,Log Family Size, and the interaction term between the two. TableVreports the results. Column (1) presents the results of a regression with no interaction terms. Across all fund starts, we find no significant effect of text-based uniqueness on expense ratio. Consistent with prior research, we also find that larger families charge significantly lower expense ratios on new funds. They have lower marginal costs for a new fund, and can cross-subsidize their new offerings with fees collected on their preexisting funds.
Next, we investigate whether the result above of no relation between expenses and uniqueness can be explained by different behavior for unique funds relative to generic funds. In column (2) of TableV, we run the same specification as in column (1), but we include the interaction betweenText UniquenessandLog Family Size. The coefficient on the noninteractedText Uniquenessterm is now positive and statistically significant, indicating that new families (whoseLog Family Sizeequals zero) that are unable to cross-subsidize charge higher initial expense ratios on unique offerings. The point estimate on the noninteractedText Uniquenessterm indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase in uniqueness is associated with a 12-bp increase in expense ratio. In contrast, the coefficient on the interaction term is negative and significant, indicating that as family size increases, the expense premium to unique funds goes down and eventually become negative. In other words, large families are more likely to cross-subsidize their unique new offerings than their generic ones.
In column (3) of TableV, we addHoldings UniquenessandReturns Uniqueness(and their interactions withLog Family Size) as explanatory variables. Similar to our findings above forText Uniqueness, we find positive coefficients on the noninteracted versions of these fundamentals-based uniqueness measures, indicating that new and small families charge higher expense ratios for funds that are more unique in terms of holdings and returns. However, unlikeText Uniqueness, the interaction terms with the fundamentals-based uniqueness measures are not negative and significant, indicating that large families do not provide any extra cross-subsidization for fundamentals-based uniqueness. In columns (4) through (6), we also add category-by-year fixed effects. The results are similar to those in the first three columns, although in column (6) the interaction betweenLog Family SizeandReturns Uniquenessis marginally significant.
In summary, TableVshows that new and small families charge higher fees on unique new offerings. Large families cross-subsidize new funds that have high text-based uniqueness, charging investors in those funds less, but do not do so for new funds that have high fundamentals-based uniqueness. This inconsistency in cross-subsidization suggests that profit maximization strategies for text-based uniqueness are different from those for fundamentals-based uniqueness. This result thus supports the hypothesis proposed in SectionIthat text-based uniqueness is more salient for investors, especially those deciding to invest in new offerings for which holdings and returns data are not yet available.
To shed additional light on fund pricing behavior, we also examine how uniqueness affects the total fees collected (in dollars). TableVIreports the results. Mutual fund sponsors should maximize the present value of expected future profits, not the rate charged. It might be profit-maximizing to initially charge a low percentage expense ratio, as large families do, if (given investor demand elasticity) this will lead to more client dollars and greater total fees collected. Attracting capital quickly is more important for unique funds due to their first-mover advantage, which is not present for generic strategies. The dependent variable in this set of tests isLog Total Expenses, the natural logarithm of the product of assets under management (in millions of dollars), and the fund's annual expense ratio. If we assume the costs (in dollars) of running a fund to be approximately fixed, this variable is highly correlated with profits to the fund sponsor.
For the first two columns of TableVI, the sample consists of all funds in their first 12 months of operation, and we regressLog Total Expenseson the three uniqueness measures (at inception),Log Family Size(at inception), and, in column (2), interaction terms. We calculate the main explanatory variables at inception for this set of tests because we want to avoid capturing any reverse effects from feedback loops (e.g., more fees leading to growth in family size and/or declining uniqueness due to new competitors).
Column (1) shows that funds with higher text-based uniqueness collect significantly more in fees in the first 12 months, even though (as we saw in TableV) they do not have higher percentage expense ratios. The point estimate onText Uniquenessis 0.21, so a one-standard-deviation increase in uniqueness is associated with a 21% increase in dollar fees collected in the first 12 months. This illustrates the main rationale for sponsors to open funds with high text-based uniqueness, the ability to attract assets quickly from potential investors with few or no other similar alternatives. In contrast, fundamentals-based uniqueness is not associated with more fees, consistent with the greater salience ofText Uniquenessfor new funds.
In column (2) of TableVI, we include interaction terms between each of the uniqueness measures andLog Family Size. We find that large families’ cross-subsidization on the expense ratio of unique new funds (that we saw in TableV) does not “cost them” dollar fees. The point estimate on the interaction term,Text Uniqueness×Log Family Size, is actually positive (although not significant), at least suggesting that large families are collecting slightly more in fees on unique products than small families. Since large families can choose whether or not to cross-subsidize, they would only do so if cross-subsidization (weakly) increased the future rents they could collect.
In columns (3) and (4), we run the same specifications as in the first two columns, but change the sample to funds that are between 13 and 36 months old. The results are similar to those in the first two columns but the point estimates onText Uniquenessare somewhat lower (0.174 in column (3) compared to 0.21 in column (1)) although still statistically significant. In columns (5) and (6), we run the same specifications as earlier, but change the sample to funds that are between 37 and 72 months old. The coefficient onText Uniquenessdecreases even more (to 0.077 in column (5)) and is no longer significant. As time rolls on, the first-mover advantage erodes and surviving unique funds’ rents are driven toward zero.
In this section, we investigate whether having a unique strategy helps fund sponsors attract clients to invest in a new fund. The results in TableVIcertainly hint at the prospect that uniqueness brings in morenewassets for recently opened funds, but assets can be inherited from another fund (that is merged into the new fund) and they can also be obtained with higher returns. We avoid these alternative possibilities by focusing on mutual fund flows, which are defined in SectionIIas the percentage change in assets under management only after controlling for returns.
Since new funds do not have a performance track record or a Morningstar rating, it would make sense for their advertised strategy (as well as the expense ratio they charge) to have a big role in determining investor decisions. First, as a simple initial test, we divide new funds into high-uniqueness (above-medianText Uniqueness) and low-uniqueness (below-medianText Uniqueness) groups and graph their cumulative flows in Figure4. Figure4, Panel A plots the average cumulative flows to each of the two groups after the inception date as well as 95% confidence intervals around each group's flows. High-uniqueness funds are able to attract more dollar flows than low-uniqueness funds in their first few years of operation. In Figure4, Panel B, we graph the difference between the two groups and its 95% confidence interval, which indicates that the difference is statistically significant. The difference increases linearly for approximately three to four years and then plateaus at a level of approximately $75 million.
We run multivariate analysis and report the results in TableVII. Using the sample of funds that are in their first 36 months of operation, we regress monthly flows on measures of uniqueness along with fund-level variables that previously shown to predict flows. In column (1), we include only theText Uniquenessmeasure as the explanatory variable of interest. We find a positive and significant coefficient on this variable. Unique new funds are able to attract more flows than new offerings that are generic. The point estimate of 0.012 indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase in uniqueness increases monthly fund flows by 1.2%, an economically significant result. In column (2), we add the fundamentals-based measures of uniqueness. The coefficient onText Uniquenessremains largely the same (i.e., positive and significant), butHoldings UniquenessandReturns Uniquenessdo not significantly predict flows. In column (3), we add category-by-month fixed effects to absorb variation in variables across different styles. We continue to get similar results. Our results are also robust to replacingFlowswith Log (1 +Flows) to make the distribution less skewed in columns (4) through (6).
Taken together, our findings in SectionIIIprovide evidence for the product differentiation theory of mutual fund innovation, and support the view that our text-based measure better captures the uniqueness that affects the behavior of fund investors and management companies than do fundamentals-based measures.
article.IV. Equilibrium Results:
While our paper's emphasis on financial innovation explains our focus on new funds, we also examine the effects of uniqueness for all funds, including those that have reached steady state. As discussed in SectionI, it is important to examine the effects of uniqueness on future fund returns. The Berk and Green (2004) model suggests that if unique strategies perform better (for a fixed level of assets), then fund investors should allocate more capital to such strategies and, through decreasing returns to scale, drive excess net returns to zero. However, if some investors prefer a certain strategy or style, preferences that are not solely based on future expected performance, then a fund offering that style could still retain those investors, even with negative expected risk-adjusted returns. The size of the investor base with each type of preference would thus determine the number of funds, and in turn the uniqueness, of each style. It is not clear how that would correlate with the magnitude of the style-specific preference, which would determine the magnitude of the (negative) excess return to each style. This is therefore an empirical question. Nonetheless, even with competition for funds, it would not be surprising for investor returns to be at least weakly negatively related to uniqueness.
In Panel A of TableVIII, the dependent variable isFund Returns (Gross), that is, mutual fund returns before subtracting fees. In column (1), we regress this performance measure onText Uniquenessand fund controls. The coefficient is negative and significant. Its value of –0.064% indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase in text-based uniqueness reduces monthly returns by 6.4 bps. In column (2), we add category-by-month fixed effects to absorb variation in performance due to Morningstar category. The point estimate is still negative, but it is much smaller in magnitude than in column (1) and is no longer statistically significant. To focus on funds that have reached equilibrium, in column (3) we restrict the sample to funds that are at least six years old. The coefficient is larger than that in column (2) but significant only at the 10% level and economically small (less than 1 bp). Overall, these results are consistent with the theoretical prediction of a (weakly) negative relation between uniqueness and fund performance.
In columns (4) through (6) of Panel A, we rerun the tests from the first three columns but also addHoldings UniquenessandReturns Uniquenessas explanatory variables. The results forText Uniquenessremain similar to those in the first three columns, with weakly negative coefficients. Consistent with the findings of Hoberg, Kumar, and Prabhala (2018), we find positive, although mostly insignificant, coefficients onHoldings Uniqueness. In contrast, the coefficients onReturns Uniquenessare negative. The reason for this discrepancy is not clear. We speculate that uniqueholdingsare likely to be less liquid and thus provide an illiquidity premium in the form of higher returns, while uniquereturnsare the result of lower weights on the market (and other factors), which might be due to higher cash positions that generate low returns. As this is not a central focus of this paper, we leave this puzzle to future research. In Panel B of TableVIII, we repeat Panel A using net returns. We find similar results and our conclusions are unchanged.
We also analyze the effect of uniqueness on the flow-performance relation. Past research finds a strong positive correlation between past risk-adjusted returns and fund flows (e.g., Sirri and Tufano (1998)). However, uniqueness could attenuate this effect if it makes benchmarking more difficult or reduces investors’ ability to move their money to other funds following the same strategy. To test this attenuation hypothesis, we regress monthly fund flows on past performance and interactions of past performance with uniqueness. We report the results in TableIX. All specifications in this table include fund control variables (as defined in TableI) and category-by-month fixed effects.
In column (1) of TableIX, we regressFlowsonText Uniqueness,Past Performance, defined here as the percentile of performance based on the last 36 months of category-adjusted returns, and their interaction. Consistent with prior research, we find a positive coefficient (of 0.043%) onPast Performance, which is highly statistically significant (t-statistic of 25.34). This point estimate means that the fund with the best past performance attracts approximately 4.3% more in monthly flows than the worst-performing fund (percentile difference of 100 × 0.043% = 4.3%). More interestingly, we find a negative and significant coefficient on the interaction term, indicating that text-based uniqueness makes flows less sensitive to past returns. The magnitude of the coefficient on the interaction term is 6.7% of the magnitude of the coefficient onPast Performance, so a one-standard-deviation increase in text-based uniqueness weakens the flow-performance relation by 6.7%.
In column (2) of TableIX, we decomposePast Performanceinto its positive component,Past Performance (+), and its negative component,Past Performance (–), and interactText Uniquenesswith these two components. The goal of this decomposition is to determine whether the attenuation of the flow-performance relation by uniqueness is symmetric or whether there is a stronger effect on inflows (after good performance) or outflows (after bad performance). We find very similar coefficients (of 0.003%) on both interaction terms, which indicates that unique funds suffer fewer outflows after performing poorly but attract fewer inflows after performing well. Thus, in equilibrium, uniqueness reduces only the volatility of flows (but not average flows), which makes it less risky for the management company to run the fund.
In column (3) of TableIX, we addHoldings Uniqueness,Returns Uniqueness, and their interactions withPast Performanceto our analysis from column (1). The negative coefficient on theText Uniquenessinteraction term remains unchanged. In contrast, the interactions with the fundamentals-based uniqueness measures have the opposite (i.e., positive) signs. This again suggests that text-based uniqueness is the characteristic that investors care about when evaluating and choosing funds based on their strategy. In columns (4) through (6), we find that the results in the first three columns are robust to defining past performance using returns from the prior 60 months instead of the prior 36 months. In summary, the results in TableIXhighlight an important motivation for offering unique funds, namely, the ability to reduce fund flow sensitivity to past performance. Smaller organizations might be offering more unique and innovative mutual fund strategies in an effort to reduce the likelihood of an outflow shock, which they are less equipped to handle than larger firms and which could lead to severe adverse effects like asset fire sales (Coval and Stafford (2007)).
article.V. Conclusion:
The mutual fund industry has witnessed significant changes in the last 40 years, with more families offering more funds in more styles. Yet, research attempting to measure fund differentiation, understand what drives strategy innovation, and examine the effects of differentiation on investor flows and fund performance is scarce. In this paper, we try to fill this gap in the literature using text from the PIS section of fund disclosures as reproduced by Morningstar.
Our paper makes several contributions. First, we show that innovation is driven by small and new families and is a function of these families’ desire to compete along a different dimension than larger families, which can beat them on fees due to economies of scale. Next, we show that opening a unique fund is associated with a more valuable real option. If there is a market for a unique strategy, it is able to attract more assets and collect more rents in its first few years of operation since few competitors offer similar strategies. Furthermore, the results are strongest using text-based measures, which demonstrate the salience of prospectus text in differentiating products and attracting investor flows.
Finally, we present evidence suggesting that uniqueness attenuates the performance-flow relation, which is useful for management companies, especially smaller firms, that are exposed to the risk that investor redemptions would force them to sell their assets at below-market prices. Furthermore, investors in steady state do not appear to give up much to obtain unique strategies. Overall, this paper improves our understanding of how fund families choose the strategies for their new fund offerings and why the total number of such strategies has proliferated so much over the last several decades.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix A: Examples of High-Uniqueness:
In this appendix, we provide the Principal Investment Strategies (PIS) (from Morningstar) of five funds that have high values of text-based uniqueness and five funds that have low values of text-based uniqueness, as of December 2015. For each fund, we also present the three uniqueness measures.
Patriot Fund
The investment seeks growth of capital. The fund generally invests in common stocks included in the Standard and Poor's 500 Index, excluding those that fail the adviser's “patriotic” investment screen. The adviser's patriotic investment screen eliminates common stocks issued by companies doing business in nations supporting terrorism (“Terror Nations”), as defined by the U.S. State Department. Currently, there are several countries designated under these authorities: Cuba, Iran, Sudan, and Syria.
Quaker Event Arbitrage Fund
The investment seeks to provide long-term growth of capital. To achieve the fund's investment objective, the Adviser invests in the securities of publicly traded companies involved in mergers, takeovers, tender offers, leveraged buyouts, spin-offs, liquidations, or similar events (“corporate reorganizations”). A variety of strategies can be employed to capitalize on the mispricing of corporate securities during corporate reorganizations, including transactions involving common and preferred stock, debt instruments, and derivative securities.
Centaur Total Return Fund
The investment seeks maximum total return through a combination of capital appreciation and current income. The fund typically invests in common stocks and other equity securities, which may include securities issued by real estate investment trusts (REITs), publicly traded master limited partnerships (MLPs) or royalty trusts, as well as preferred stocks, convertible bonds, convertible preferred stocks, and warrants. In addition to investing in equity securities that offer high dividend yields, the Advisor expects to generate income from selling covered call options on securities in the fund. It is nondiversified.
MFS Utilities Fund
The investment seeks total return. The fund normally invests at least 80% of its net assets in securities of issuers in the utilities industry. Issuers in the utilities industry include issuers engaged in the manufacture, production, generation, transmission, sale or distribution of electric, gas or other types of energy, and issuers engaged in telecommunications, including wireless, telephone, and cable (but not engaged in public broadcasting). It primarily invests its assets in equity securities, but may also invest in debt instruments, including less than investment grade quality debt instruments.
Dreyfus Third Century Fund
The investment seeks to provide capital growth, with current income as a secondary goal. The fund invests at least 80% of its net assets in the common stocks of companies that, in the opinion of the fund's management, meet traditional investment standards and conduct their business in a manner that contributes to the enhancement of the quality of life in America. Its investment strategy combines a disciplined investment process that consists of computer modeling techniques, fundamental analysis, and risk management with a social investment process.
RS Small Cap Equity Fund
The investment seeks long-term capital appreciation. The fund normally invests at least 80% of its net assets in equity securities of small-capitalization companies, which may include common stocks, preferred stocks, or other securities convertible into common stock. Its investment team considers a company to be a small-capitalization company if its market capitalization is either up to $3 billion or 120% of the market capitalization of the largest company included in the Russell 2000 Index. The fund typically invests most of its equity assets in securities of U.S. companies but may also invest any portion of its assets in foreign securities.
John Hancock Funds II: Real Estate Securities Fund
The investment seeks to achieve a combination of long-term capital appreciation and current income. The fund invests at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes) in equity securities of REITs and real estate companies. Equity securities include common stock, preferred stock, and securities convertible into common stock. It may invest up to 10% of its total assets in securities of foreign real estate companies. The fund is nondiversified.
Baron Asset Fund
The investment seeks capital appreciation through long-term investments primarily in securities of midsized companies with undervalued assets or favorable growth prospects. The fund invests for the long term primarily in equity securities in the form of common stock of midsized growth companies. BAMCO, Inc. (“BAMCO” or the “Adviser”) defines midsized companies as those, at the time of purchase, with market capitalizations above $2.5 billion or the smallest market cap stock in the Russell Midcap Growth Index at reconstitution, whichever is larger, and below the largest market cap stock in the Russell Midcap Growth Index at reconstitution.
Diamond Hill Small Cap Fund
The investment seeks to provide long-term capital appreciation. The fund normally invests at least 80% of its net assets in U.S. equity securities with small market capitalizations that the Adviser believes are undervalued. Small cap companies are defined as companies with market capitalizations at the time of purchase below $2.5 billion or in the range of those market capitalizations of companies included in the Russell 2000 Index at the time of purchase. The Adviser focuses on estimating a company's value independent of its current stock price.
Fidelity Select Portfolios: Consumer Discretionary Portfolio
The investment seeks capital appreciation. The fund invests primarily in common stocks. It invests at least 80% of assets in securities of companies principally engaged in the manufacture and distribution of consumer discretionary products and services. The fund invests in domestic and foreign issuers. It uses fundamental analysis of factors such as each issuer's financial condition and industry position, as well as market and economic conditions to select investments. The fund is nondiversified.
article.Appendix B: Process for Filtering Out Ir:
One of the biggest problems with reliance on text is the large number of common nonfinancial words (such as “the,” “is,” or “and”) and/or boilerplate language used in legal texts such as prospectuses, which only add noise to any analysis. In this appendix, we describe the process we use to remove words that are unlikely to be relevant for describing fund strategy.
Mutual fund names are used to communicate a fund's style or strategy to investors. However, unlike the PIS section of the prospectus, fund names have very limited space so they need to distill the fund's description to the most informative words. We therefore use the frequency of words in all fund names (from the universe of all funds in CRSP) as a measure of relevance. In creating this dictionary, we first eliminate brand names, that is, words in a fund's name that are also in the fund family's name and/or the name of the advisory or subadvisory firm that manages the fund (e.g., Blackrock, iShares). For all remaining words, we generate a dictionary of each word with the frequency of that word. We do the same for all words in all the PIS texts using the data from Morningstar.
For each word, we calculate an information ratio equal to the frequency of the word in the PIS texts divided by the frequency of the word in the fund names. Words that occur often in the PIS texts relative to their rate of occurrence in fund names are likely to be less relevant and should be dropped. However, we need to determine a proper cutoff in the information ratio for dropping a specific word. The cutoff point should be chosen to maximize the statistical power of the outcome variable (Text Overlap) in a predetermined validity test.
Our validity test is based on the idea that two funds in the same Morningstar category should have higher textual overlap than two funds that are in different categories. As we lower the information ratio cutoff, irrelevant words are eliminated, leading to a more statistically significant difference between within-style and across-style overlaps. If we drop it too much, however, we start to lose informative words, leading to a reduction in precision and statistical significance. This is exactly the pattern we see in TableBI, as thet-statistic on the difference betweenAverage Overlap(average text overlap across all funds) andAverage Overlap Within(average text overlap with funds in the same category) increases as we lower the information ratio cutoff, and then decreases. We choose 75 as the cutoff in the information ratio since this cutoff maximizes thet-statistic on the difference between average overlap and average within-style overlap. Using this cutoff, we remove 1,045 words (of a total of 7,072 words) that appear at least 75 times more in the PIS text than they do in the fund name.9
The investment seeksgrowthofcapital. The fund generally invests in commonstocksincluded in the Standard and Poor's 500Index, excluding those thatfailthe adviser's “patriotic” investmentscreen. The adviser'spatrioticinvestmentscreen eliminatescommonstocksissued bycompaniesdoing business innationssupportingterrorism(“Terror Nations”), as defined by theU.S.StateDepartment. Currently, there are several countries designated under theseauthorities:Cuba,Iran,Sudan
,andSyria.
The underlined words are much more relevant for identifying the unique nature of this fund than the rest of the text, which is filtered out.
article.Appendix C: Textual Analysis of PIS (fro:
In this appendix, we provide definitions for text-based measures of readability and tonality and present summary statistics for these measures for the PIS text for different subsamples of mutual funds from 2000 to 2015. We calculate each fund-year measure based on the exact text provided by Morningstar, without the formatting and filtering described in AppendixBof the paper. For each fund's PIS, we calculate three measures of readability. First, we constructFlesch Reading Ease, which is defined as 206.835 – 1.015 × (#words/#sentences) – 84.6 × (#syllables/#words) (Flesch (1948)). Next, we calculate theFog Index, which is defined as 0.4 × (#words/#sentences + #wordswith3ormoresyllables/#words) (Gunning (1952)). We multiply theFog Indexby negative one so that larger numbers correspond tomorereadable text. Finally, we construct aPlain Englishmeasure, which is the simple average of six standardized components: average sentence length, average word length, passive voice, legalese, personal pronouns, and negative/superfluous phrases (see Loughran and McDonald (2014) for details). We also calculate the incidence of positive, neutral, and negative words in each fund's PIS using the Loughran-McDonald (2014) sentiment word list.10Finally, we count and report the total number of words in each fund's PIS.
In Panel A of TableCI, we report average values of each measure for funds sorted into terciles byText: Average Overlap, as well as the average difference between the third and first terciles in column (4), with thet-statistic on the difference in brackets in column (5). In Panel B, we report average values of each measure for three sample subperiods, as well as the average difference between the third and first subperiod in column (4), with thet-statistic on the difference in brackets in column (5).
All three measures of readability are significantly higher for funds that have higher textual overlap with other funds (i.e., lower textual uniqueness). This finding is consistent with unique funds using strategies that are more complex and harder to explain, and/or unique funds being marketed to more sophisticated investors who can understand more complex language structure. In addition, words related to tone, whether positive, negative, or uncertain, are all significantly less common in funds with high textual overlap (lower uniqueness). This result could mean that unique funds are more likely to describe hypothetical performance outcomes than less unique ones, but the average incidence of tonal words is low (2% to 3%, or one to two words, in the average text) so any differences are not likely to be economically significant. Finally, consistent with the readability results, mutual funds with higher textual overlap (lower uniqueness) use fewer words.
We also find in Panel B that readability has declined over time, notwithstanding SEC efforts to improve readability of financial disclosures. Similarly, the number of words in the Morningstar summary of the PIS has increased from around 67 words in the first third of the sample period (2000 to 2004) to 79 words in the last third (2011 to 2015). However, we see only Morningstar's version of the PIS, not the actual version, so this increase could be explained by changes in Morningstar's policy (i.e., how many words or characters they want to have in this field) in addition to changes made by fund sponsors. Finally, the use of tonality has declined over time, especially the incidence of uncertain words, which fell by almost one-third from the first third to the last third of the sample period.
